subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Logical progression from introduction through modelling cases to conclusion
Well-organized structure with clear headings, table of contents, and cross-referencing
Relevant content throughout with minimal digressions
weaknesses: Minor typographical and formatting inconsistencies (e.g. âWrongâ vs âFrontâ, variable styles)
Some repeated material (definitions, conclusions) reduces conciseness
References use varied formats; a consistent citation style is needed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report is coherent and shows clear logical development with a recognizable introductionâbodyâconclusion structure. Headings, a contents page and accurate cross-references support reader navigation. Almost all content directly addresses the research question. Minor lapses (typos, formatting inconsistencies, occasional repetition) prevent a top mark but place the work solidly at level 3 out of 4.

strengths: Variables are defined and standard notation is used consistently for most of the exploration
Effective use of diagrams, algebraic expressions and Desmos graphs to complement the narrative
Explanations generally match the mathematics and maintain clarity
weaknesses: Notation inconsistencies (e.g. switching between I and Ï, use of n instead of v)
Graphs often lack labeled axes, units and legends, reducing interpretability
Minor label errors (e.g. W_rong vs W_front) and unit switches (kg/g) disrupt full consistency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and notation are mostly appropriate, and the student uses multiple representations effectively to visualise the problem. Communication is clear and largely consistent, despite occasional slips in notation and graph annotation. These characteristics merit level 3 out of 4 for Mathematical Communication.

strengths: Use of personal parameters (body dimensions, meteorological data) shows initiative beyond textbook examples
Three distinct wind scenarios reflect clear personal engagement and real-world relevance
Interdisciplinary connections (physics, geography) indicate genuine individual investment
weaknesses: Mathematical methods (basic kinematics, simple differentiation) remain standard
Structure follows a conventional IA format without novel experimentation
No advanced or unconventional techniques employed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration demonstrates significant independent thinking and a clear personal approach through realistic choices and scenario design. Creativity is evident in selecting and justifying parameters. However, the techniques are standard and the personal approach, while clear, does not reach the highest band. The work aligns with level 2 out of 3 for Personal Engagement.

strengths: Reflections identify strengths, limitations and propose extensions throughout the conclusion
Some critical analysis of model assumptions (cuboid simplification, risks of running fast)
Clear linkage between mathematical findings and real-life decision-making
weaknesses: Reflection is largely descriptive with limited deeper critical probing
No quantitative sensitivity or error analysis to support critique
Proposed extensions lack prioritization or clear expected impact
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation section provides meaningful reflection and basic critique, with a clear connection to enhanced understanding of the problem. Yet it stops short of the substantial critical reflection and deep analysis required for the top band. Consequently, the work fits level 2 out of 3 for Reflection.

strengths: Sophisticated application of vector decomposition, parametric modelling and calculus within the IA context
Comprehensive inclusion of all rain-impact components (W_top, W_front, W_back) for rigorous comparison
Insightful derivation of threshold velocity conditions that demonstrate thorough analytical thinking
weaknesses: Several minor unit inconsistencies (kg vs g) and arithmetic simplification errors
Occasional sign and factor mistakes in derivative steps
Early omissions in derivations (e.g. travel-time factor) require later correction
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite some computational slips, the exploration applies a rich suite of HL-level mathematics with rigourâvector analysis, elementary optimisation and parametric equationsâto address the research question. The modelling of three wind cases, full derivations of rain-impact functions and critical insights into optimal speeds exhibit a high degree of sophistication and thorough understanding. This justifies awarding the maximum 6 out of 6 for Use of Mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: Choosing personal body dimensions, selecting realistic meteorological data and devising three wind scenarios show noticeable independent decision-making beyond textbook examples.  The mathematical techniques themselves (basic kinematics, trig components, simple differentiation) remain standard, so the evidence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ.

The student demonstrates a sound grasp of component velocities, rateâtime relationships and basic optimisation, and can explain why different wind angles alter the optimal speed.  However, misunderstandings of intensity units and the unchanged constant in front-rain for \(\theta=90^\circ\) suggest understanding is good âin partsâ rather than thorough throughout.

Derivations of \(W_{top}\) and \(W_{front}\) are mostly correct, and graphs match formulas.  Nonetheless, unit inconsistencies (kg versus g), a sign error in the derivative expression, and occasional mis-labelled variables indicate that accuracy is not flawless, fitting the âmostly correctâ descriptor.

Almost all text, diagrams and calculations relate directly to the research question; tangential remarks are limited to short digressions (e.g.
MythBusters anecdote, safety comment).  However, the write-up is lengthy and sometimes repeats ideas (for example, the velocity definition and the graphical behaviour of similar functions), so it cannot be called âhighly conciseâ.  The work therefore fits the ârelevant and mostly conciseâ descriptor.

Variables are defined, units are usually stated and standard notation (\(v,\;u,\;\theta\)) is employed consistently across cases.  Nevertheless, occasional slips occur: the intensity is first labelled \(I\) then referred to as a density, and boldface appears sporadically.  The overall use is therefore âmostly appropriateâ rather than âconsistent and accurateâ.

Explanations generally match the mathematics shown, and the narrative is understandable.  Small inconsistencies (e.g.
calling \(W_{front}\) âWrongâ in a heading, switching between kg and g mid-equation) detract from full consistency, keeping the strand in the âclear and mostly consistentâ band.

Vector decomposition, parametric equations, graphical modelling and elementary calculus are all well within HL AI course level and are applied purposely to address the aim.  The mathematics is more than superficial substitution but does not reach a level of sophistication (e.g.
multivariable optimisation, stochastic modelling) that would warrant bands 5â6.  Hence band 4 is appropriate.

The student provides diagrams of the cuboid model, several Desmos graphs and algebraic formulae.  These representations complement one another and help the reader visualise the situation.  The graphs, however, are largely raw plots without scales or annotations explaining key features, so the use is effective but not âsophisticatedâ.

The evaluation section identifies strengths, limitations and possible extensions, and reflections are interwoven in conclusions (e.g.
linking results to real-life decisions).  While thoughtful, the discussion remains fairly descriptive; deeper probing of model assumptions or alternative formulations is limited, placing it in the âmeaningful reflection throughoutâ band.

Incorporating three distinct wind directions, estimating realistic input data and using Desmos graphs display creativity and initiative beyond a minimal treatment.  The originality is solid but not exceptionalâno novel mathematical techniques or experimental validation have been attemptedâso the strand fits the middle band.

The exploration follows a clear progression from introduction, through three successive modelling situations, to conclusion and evaluation.  Each section builds naturally on the previous one, enabling the reader to follow the argument without major jumps.  Minor lapses (e.g.	ypo in the derivative, repeated wording) interrupt the flow but do not prevent understanding, so the work reaches the âcoherent and clear logical developmentâ standard rather than the âexcellentâ level.

The student comments on the impact of treating the human as a cuboid, the danger of running fast, and the impossibility of experimental control, demonstrating some critique of the model.  However, no quantitative sensitivity tests or rigorous error analysis are performed, so the work shows âmeaningful analysis with some critiqueâ rather than âdeep critical analysisâ.

The reflections explicitly state how the mathematics enhanced the studentâs understanding of physics and real-life decision making, and propose practical applications.  The link to learning is clear, but not extensively developed or evidenced, awarding the middle band.

Headings, a table of contents and numbered sub-sections give the report a recognisable introductionâbodyâconclusion structure.  Cross-referencing of graphs and equations is generally accurate.  Transitions between the three wind cases are clear, although some material (e.g.
repetition of definitions) could have been merged.  Overall organisation is therefore âwell organisedâ rather than âexceptional and seamlessâ.

The modelling approach is clearly personal: the student frames a day-to-day question, selects their own parameters and drives the investigation.  While the structure is still conventional, this personalised framing satisfies the âclear personal approach that drives the explorationâ descriptor.

annotations.comment: References display varied formats for authors and dates; adopt a consistent citation style (e.g. APA or MLA) throughout the list.
The title is descriptive but somewhat verbose; consider streamlining it to improve clarity and conciseness while retaining key terms.
The table of contents is comprehensive but could be condensed by grouping similar sections to improve overall organization and reader navigation.
The rationale relies heavily on a popular anecdote but lacks deeper physical justification; expand on the mechanics of rain impact and time trade-off to enrich the context.
The aim effectively frames the mathematical modeling objective and links it to real-world decision making, providing clear direction for the exploration.
Modeling the person as a cuboid is a sound simplification that is later acknowledged in the evaluation, demonstrating thoughtful abstraction in the mathematical model.
The travel time formula uses ânâ instead of the defined velocity variable âvâ; this inconsistency can cause confusion, so align notation throughout.
The conversion of 3.5 mm/h to m/s is clearly shown. To further enhance precision, include units at each step of the calculation.
The choice of rainfall velocity and intensity is grounded in literature and demonstrates initiative in realistic parameter selection.
The initial form of the W_top equation omits the travel time factor; integrate the \(d/v\) term from the start to maintain coherence in the derivation.
In the W_front formula, the intensity symbol changes between \(I\) and \(\rho\); unify notation and specify units to avoid ambiguity.
The arithmetic simplification to 0.0972 g appears inconsistent with earlier factors; re-calculate the numeric coefficient carefully.
Variable formatting (e.g. boldface \(v\), \(I\)) is inconsistent; adopt a single style for all symbols to improve readability.
The expression for W_front introduces \(x\) instead of the velocity \(v\), breaking notational consistency; align variable names.
The derivation of the 1000 m world record speed uses an unclear sum for time; provide the precise competition time rather than \(60+60+11\) s.
The heading âWrongâ appears to be a typo in place of âFrontâ; correct this label to maintain professional presentation.
The derivative \(\frac{dw_{total}}{dv}\) contains sign and factor mistakes; revisit each differentiation step to ensure mathematical correctness.
The claim that the topârain correlation remains unchanged at \(\theta=45^\circ\) would benefit from an algebraic justification rather than a numerical observation.
Graph 6 is effective but lacks labeled axes and units; adding clear axis titles will greatly enhance interpretability.
The derivative in Equation 5 includes both \(\sin\theta\) and \(\cos\theta\) incorrectly; re-derive to confirm which function is appropriate.
Notation âW_{\text{rong}}â appears in the heading, likely intended as âW_{\text{front}}â; standardize labels to avoid misinterpretation.
The derivative of W_front in Equation 2 should account for the product with the velocity term; explicitly expand before differentiating for full accuracy.
The conclusions on page 16 repeat earlier observations; consider consolidating to maintain conciseness and avoid redundancy.
The third scenario discussion lacks a supporting plot for W_top at \(\theta=135^\circ\); include the corresponding graph for completeness.
Identifying the velocity threshold where \(ucos\theta + v = 0\) yields a critical insight into avoiding frontal rain; this is a strong mathematical observation.
The evaluation that W_front contributes minimally under certain conditions demonstrates critical appreciation of each componentâs influence on total exposure.
Incorporation of W_back rounds out the model effectively by recognizing rearward rain impact, enhancing the comprehensiveness of the study.
The equation containing symbol âÎ²â is unclear and appears incorrect; replace with an explicit relationship in familiar mathematical notation.
The insight that a specific speed can avoid both front and back rain entirely is excellent and shows deep understanding of the modelâs implications.
Graph 15 would benefit from a legend distinguishing \(W_{top}, W_{front},\) and \(W_{back}\); this will help the reader follow multi-component comparisons.
The exploration draws strong interdisciplinary connections to physics and geography, reflecting genuine personal engagement and broader impact.
The suggested extensions are thoughtful; prioritizing them or indicating expected impact would enhance the critical evaluation and planning.
